headline: Hormuz Shock Looms Over Markets as Cyclicals Close the Week on Top
body_html:
Friday's session closed on a constructive note for cyclical names, with Consumer and Industrials sectors leading the advance — up 1.13% and 0.97% respectively — while breadth tilted positive at 225 advancers against 167 decliners across a 396-name universe. Wood-products names commanded attention: Boise Cascade surged 5.29% and timber REIT Weyerhaeuser added 4.22%, signalling renewed appetite for building-materials exposure heading into summer. Footwear and apparel joined the rally, with Crocs gaining 4.44% and Abercrombie & Fitch up 4.30%.

The technology complex told a sharply different story. Software and Semiconductors each shed 0.46%–0.47% on the day, dragged by steep single-name losses: Okta fell 6.86%, CrowdStrike dropped 5.66%, Zscaler lost 5.34%, and MongoDB slid 5.73%. The pattern suggests continued rotation out of high-multiple software into economically sensitive sectors rather than any broad risk-off move.

The defining macro development over the weekend, however, is the eruption of Middle East hostilities. The United States conducted strikes against roughly 140 Iranian military targets, and Tehran has declared the Strait of Hormuz closed until further notice. With a critical global energy chokepoint in dispute, energy markets, shipping rates, and defence-linked equities will be in sharp focus when trading resumes Monday morning.

briefs.headline: Strait of Hormuz Declared Closed as US-Iran Confrontation Escalates
briefs.body: Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy announced the closure of the Strait of Hormuz to maritime traffic following US military strikes on Iranian targets over the weekend. Tehran simultaneously struck Gulf states in retaliation, raising the prospect of sustained disruption to one of the world's most critical oil-transit corridors. Crude and freight markets will price the risk premium when they open.

briefs.headline: US Conducted Strikes on Roughly 140 Iranian Military Sites
briefs.body: Washington confirmed it launched strikes targeting approximately 140 Iranian military installations, citing an attack on a Cyprus-flagged container vessel as the trigger. President Trump separately warned of further missile action should Iran threaten US personnel. The exchange marks the sharpest direct US-Iran military confrontation in years and places Gulf security squarely at the top of the week's geopolitical agenda.

briefs.headline: Container Ship Crew Abandons Vessel Near Oman After Fire
briefs.body: The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations reported that the crew of a container ship was forced to abandon the vessel near Oman following an onboard fire. The incident occurred amid the broader escalation in the region and underscores the mounting operational risk for commercial shipping transiting the Arabian Sea and Gulf of Oman approaches.

briefs.headline: Iran's Supreme Leader Vows Retribution Following Leadership Losses
briefs.body: Iran's supreme leader publicly pledged revenge following the deaths of senior figures, including the former emir of Qatar Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani — who died at 74 — adding a diplomatic dimension to the crisis. Markets will watch whether Qatar's role as a regional intermediary is affected, given its historical importance in US-Iran back-channel communications.

briefs.headline: Washington Presses Tehran for Guaranteed Strait Access Amid Talks
briefs.body: US officials are actively pressing Iran to restore freedom of navigation through the Strait of Hormuz, with negotiations reportedly centred on the strait's status. Any diplomatic breakthrough that reopens the waterway could swiftly relieve the energy-price premium, while a prolonged standoff would sustain pressure on transport costs and oil supply chains globally.

briefs.headline: India and New Zealand Cement Strategic Partnership With Maritime Focus
briefs.body: India and New Zealand announced a new strategic partnership with maritime security as a central pillar, a development that takes on added significance given the simultaneous closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The agreement signals a broader Indo-Pacific push to diversify naval cooperation and secure alternative sea-lane arrangements as Gulf tensions intensify.
